context_text: Right, that's how long it takes, not a day less,—Qfwfq said,—once, as I went past, I drew a sign at a point in space, just so I could find it again two hundred million years later, when we went by the next time around.

subject: we

context_text: At a rough guess, I calculated we had completed half of our galactic revolution: I had only to be patient, the second half always seemed to go by more quickly.

subject: we

context_text: Had we already gone past it?
normalized_context_text: Had we already gone past it?
rendered_context_text: Had we already gone past it?
subject: we

context_text: My sign had perhaps remained who knows where, behind, completely remote from the revolutionary orbit of our system.
normalized_context_text: My sign had perhaps remained who knows where, behind, completely remote from the revolutionary orbit of our system.
original_context_text: My sign had perhaps remained who knows where, behind, completely remote from the revolutionary orbit of [[our]] system.
rendered_context_text: My sign had perhaps remained who knows where, behind, completely remote from the revolutionary orbit of our system.
subject: we

context_text: For about a hundred millennia I tormented myself, going over my calculations: it turned out that our course touched that spot not every galactic year but only every three, that is, every six hundred million solar years.
normalized_context_text: For about a hundred millennia I tormented myself, going over my calculations: it turned out that our course touched that spot not every galactic year but only every three, that is, every six hundred million solar years.

subject: we

context_text: When I finally raised my eyes (in the meanwhile, sight had begun in our world, and, as a result, also life), I saw what I would never have expected to see.
normalized_context_text: When I finally raised my eyes (in the meanwhile, sight had begun in our world, and, as a result, also life), I saw what I would never have expected to see.
original_context_text: When I finally raised my eyes (in the meanwhile, sight had begun in [[our]] world, and, as a result, also life), I saw what I would never have expected to see.

subject: we

context_text: Finally, a plurimillennial chain of deductions led me to the solution: on another planetary system which perfonned its galactic revolution before us, there was a certain Kgwgk (the name I deduced afterwards, in the later era of names), a spiteful type, consumed with envy, who had erased my sign in a vandalistic impulse and then, with vulgar artifice, had attempted to make another.
normalized_context_text: Finally, a plurimillennial chain of deductions led me to the solution: on another planetary system which perfonned its galactic revolution before us, there was a certain Kgwgk (the name I deduced afterwards, in the later era of names), a spiteful type, consumed with envy, who had erased my sign in a vandalistic impulse and then, with vulgar artifice, had attempted to make another.

subject: we

context_text: Now things were different, however, because the world, as I mentioned, was beginning to produce an image of itself, and in everything a form was beginning to correspond to a function, and the forms of that time, we believed, had a long future ahead of them (instead, we were wrong: take—to give you a fairly recent example—the dinosaurs), and therefore in this new sign of mine you could perceive the influence of our new way of looking at things, call it style if you like, that special way that everything had to be, there, in a certain fashion.

context_text: But in the duration of that galactic year we already began to realize that the world's forms had been temporary up until then, and that they would change, one by one.

subject: we

context_text: In other words, I was ashamed of that sign which went on through the centuries, being passed by worlds in flight, making a ridiculous spectacle of itself and of me and of that temporary way we had had of seeing things.

subject: we

context_text: The idea of fixing with signs the points where we passed—as it had come to me and to Kgwgk—had occurred to many, scattered over billions of planets of other solar systems, and I was constantly running into one of these things, or a pair, or even a dozen, simple two-dimensional scrawls, or else three-dimensional solids (polyhedrons, for example), or even things constructed with more care, with the fourth dimension and everything.
